software.definition: Commercial and internally developed digital products that process information, automate workflows, manage infrastructure, enable transactions, support decision-making, or facilitate digital interactions across consumers, enterprises, and public institutions. This umbrella market includes both horizontal software used across industries and vertical software built for specialized operational environments.
software.expandedDefinition:
Commercial and internally developed digital products that process information, automate workflows, manage infrastructure, enable transactions, support decision-making, or facilitate digital interactions across consumers, enterprises, and public institutions. This umbrella market spans both horizontal software used across industries and vertical software built for specialized operating environments, but it excludes hardware and pure professional services.

At the top of the taxonomy, this category is intentionally broad: it captures software as a product class rather than a use case or an industry. The practical boundary is that the offering must be a repeatable software product, platform, or subscription service that can be sold, licensed, or deployed independently of custom implementation work. Software products are differentiated by the workflow they own, the users they serve, and whether they operate as a system of record, system of engagement, control plane, or infrastructure layer.

development & deployment.definition: The software layer used by engineering, platform, and IT teams to build, test, integrate, deploy, operate, and improve software systems. This category sits between the application layer and the infrastructure layer, and it includes the tools that shape software delivery speed, reliability, security, and maintainability across modern development environments.
development & deployment.expandedDefinition:
The software layer used by engineering, platform, and IT teams to build, test, integrate, deploy, operate, and improve software systems and data/AI workloads. This category sits between the application layer and the infrastructure layer, and it includes the tools that shape release velocity, delivery reliability, code quality, and runtime automation across modern development environments.

Products in this layer are typically purchased by developers, DevOps teams, platform engineers, and application owners to reduce friction in software creation and delivery. The market includes coding tools, build and release automation, testing systems, developer collaboration, and operational tooling for software delivery. It is distinct from production infrastructure because it helps teams create and ship software rather than host or run the business application itself.

applications.definition: End-user-facing software used to support business, operational, analytical, collaborative, financial, customer-facing, and industry-specific workflows. This layer includes software that owns a user workflow or business process directly, rather than providing underlying infrastructure, developer tooling, or system connectivity.
applications.expandedDefinition:
End-user-facing software used to support business, operational, analytical, collaborative, financial, customer-facing, and industry-specific workflows. This layer includes products that own a user workflow or business process directly, rather than providing underlying infrastructure, developer tooling, or system connectivity.

Applications are typically evaluated on how well they help employees or external users complete a task, coordinate a process, make a decision, or transact with a business. Within the taxonomy, this layer is split into horizontal application families and vertical industry applications. The distinction matters because application markets usually compete on user experience, process depth, domain fit, and business workflow ownership rather than on underlying technical infrastructure.

infrastructure software.definition: Foundational software layers that provision, secure, monitor, connect, store, and optimize the technical environments on which applications run. Infrastructure software is typically purchased by IT, security, cloud, and platform teams to improve runtime reliability, scalability, observability, resilience, and operational control.
infrastructure software.expandedDefinition:
Foundational software layers that provision, secure, monitor, connect, store, and optimize the technical environments on which applications run. Infrastructure software is typically purchased by IT, security, cloud, and platform teams to improve reliability, scalability, observability, resilience, and operational control.

These products are not the business application itself; they are the enabling stack underneath it. The market includes compute and storage, networking, databases, backup and recovery, IT operations, security, and related control-plane capabilities. Competitive differentiation usually comes from performance, automation, fault tolerance, integration depth, and how well the software supports hybrid and distributed environments.

analytics and business intelligence soft.definition: Software used to aggregate, analyze, visualize, and report on data to support operational and strategic decision-making.
analytics and business intelligence soft.expandedDefinition:
Software used to aggregate, model, analyze, visualize, and operationalize structured and unstructured data to support operational, tactical, and strategic decision-making across an organization. Products in this market enable business users, analysts, and executives to monitor performance, identify trends, investigate business drivers, and generate actionable insights from enterprise data.

Core capabilities typically include dashboards, reporting, ad hoc querying, KPI tracking, data visualization, OLAP analysis, semantic modeling, embedded analytics, natural language querying, and self-service analytics interfaces. More advanced platforms may also incorporate augmented analytics, AI-assisted insight generation, forecasting, anomaly detection, and real-time operational analytics.

The defining characteristic of this segment is that the software sits at the analytical consumption and decision-support layer of the data stack. Unlike data management software, which focuses on storing, transforming, governing, and moving data, analytics and BI platforms are primarily used to interpret and present information to business users in a consumable and actionable form. Similarly, unlike operational applications that execute workflows directly, BI software is generally used to inform decisions, monitor operations, and evaluate performance across functions such as finance, sales, marketing, operations, and executive management.

Products in this category are typically purchased by business intelligence teams, data and analytics organizations, finance teams, operational leaders, and executive stakeholders. Competitive differentiation is often driven by ease of use, visualization quality, semantic modeling flexibility, scalability, real-time analytics performance, governance capabilities, and integration with broader enterprise data ecosystems.

data management software.definition: Software used to ingest, store, transform, govern, organize, and operationalize enterprise data assets across databases, warehouses, lakes, and pipelines. This market includes the tooling that creates trusted, accessible, and usable data foundations for analytics, applications, and AI systems, rather than the analytics or application layers that consume the data. Typical capabilities include data integration, ETL/ELT, metadata management, governance, lineage, data quality, master data management, replication, and database administration.
data management software.expandedDefinition:
Software used to ingest, integrate, transform, organize, govern, and operationalize enterprise data across applications, analytics environments, and AI systems. Products in this market manage how data moves through the enterprise, how it is standardized and governed, and how it is prepared for downstream consumption by applications, business users, and machine learning systems.

Core capabilities typically include ETL and ELT pipelines, data integration, data orchestration, metadata management, data lineage, data quality monitoring, master data management, cataloging, governance workflows, replication, schema management, and data preparation. Many platforms also support workflow automation, policy enforcement, observability, and cross-environment synchronization across cloud, on-premise, and hybrid data architectures.

The defining characteristic of Data Management Software is that it governs the lifecycle, movement, usability, and control of data across systems rather than serving as the operational database where applications directly store and retrieve runtime data. These products sit above and around databases, warehouses, lakes, and operational systems to create trusted, interoperable, and reusable enterprise data environments.

Data Management Software is distinct from:

Database Infrastructure, which provides the underlying runtime data storage and query engine itself
Analytics and Business Intelligence Software, which consumes prepared data to generate reporting and insights
Integration and Orchestration Middleware, which focuses on cross-system workflow coordination rather than long-term data governance and management

Products in this segment are typically purchased by data engineering teams, analytics organizations, platform teams, and enterprise IT functions. Competitive differentiation is often driven by scalability, interoperability, governance depth, pipeline automation, metadata intelligence, data quality capabilities, and support for modern distributed data architectures.

application development and testing soft.definition: Software used to design, code, test, debug, version, and maintain applications throughout the software development lifecycle.
application development and testing soft.expandedDefinition:
Software used by developers, engineering teams, and IT organizations to design, build, test, debug, secure, deploy, and maintain software applications throughout the software development lifecycle (SDLC). This market encompasses the tooling, environments, and platforms that enable organizations to create and continuously improve software products across web, mobile, desktop, cloud, and embedded environments.

Core capabilities typically include integrated development environments (IDEs), source code management, version control, collaborative coding, build automation, CI/CD pipelines, automated testing, quality assurance, debugging, application performance testing, developer security testing, release management, and software lifecycle management. Many platforms also support code review workflows, dependency management, containerized development environments, and AI-assisted software development capabilities such as code generation and developer copilots.

The defining characteristic of this market is that the software is used primarily by engineering and development teams to create and maintain software itself, rather than to operate business workflows or production infrastructure. These products sit within the software engineering toolchain and are tightly integrated into developer workflows, release processes, and application lifecycle governance.

Within the market, major subsegments often include:

Developer tooling (IDEs, coding environments, repositories)
DevOps and CI/CD tooling (build, deployment, release automation)
Application testing software (functional, regression, performance, and security testing)
Application lifecycle management (ALM) and developer collaboration tools
Developer productivity and observability tooling
AI-assisted software development platforms

This category is distinct from:

Infrastructure software, which manages runtime compute, storage, and networking environments
IT operations management software, which monitors and operates production systems after deployment
Integration and orchestration middleware, which connects applications and workflows
AI infrastructure software, which is specifically focused on developing and operationalizing AI models rather than general-purpose application development

Products in this market are typically purchased by engineering organizations, platform teams, DevOps teams, and CIO/CTO organizations. Competitive differentiation is often driven by developer productivity gains, ecosystem integration, automation depth, scalability, collaboration support, deployment reliability, security integration, and support for modern cloud-native and AI-driven development workflows.

integration and orchestration middleware.definition: Software that connects applications, systems, APIs, and workflows while coordinating data flows and process execution across environments.
integration and orchestration middleware.expandedDefinition:
Software used to connect applications, systems, APIs, databases, services, and workflows while coordinating data exchange and process execution across heterogeneous technology environments. Products in this market enable organizations to integrate fragmented systems, automate cross-platform workflows, synchronize data, and manage interactions between applications operating across cloud, on-premise, hybrid, and edge environments.

Core capabilities typically include API management, application integration, enterprise service buses (ESBs), workflow orchestration, event streaming, message queuing, data synchronization, low-code integration tooling, robotic process orchestration, and integration-platform-as-a-service (iPaaS) functionality. More advanced platforms may also support real-time event processing, service mesh coordination, process automation, distributed workflow execution, and AI-driven orchestration capabilities.

The defining characteristic of this market is that the software acts as the connective and coordination layer between systems rather than serving as the system of record or primary business application itself. These products are primarily designed to enable interoperability, process continuity, and workflow automation across otherwise disconnected enterprise environments.

Within enterprise architectures, integration and orchestration middleware often serves several distinct functions:

Application integration — connecting SaaS, on-premise, legacy, and cloud applications
API management — exposing, securing, governing, and monitoring APIs
Workflow orchestration — coordinating multi-step business or technical processes across systems
Event and message management — enabling asynchronous communication and real-time data exchange
Automation enablement — reducing manual intervention across operational workflows

This market is distinct from:

Application development software, which focuses on creating software applications themselves
IT operations management software, which monitors and operates infrastructure environments
ERP or operational applications, which directly own business workflows and transactional systems of record
Data management software, which focuses on storing, governing, and transforming data rather than coordinating application interactions

Products in this market are typically purchased by enterprise architecture teams, integration teams, platform engineering organizations, IT operations groups, and digital transformation leaders. Competitive differentiation is often driven by integration breadth, connector ecosystems, scalability, workflow flexibility, low-code capabilities, API governance, real-time processing performance, and ease of integrating legacy and modern cloud-native systems.

ai infrastructure and operations softwar.definition: Software used to build, deploy, operate, monitor, govern, and scale AI systems and machine learning workloads. This category includes the infrastructure and operational tooling that sits beneath AI-enabled applications, supporting model development, inference serving, orchestration, observability, governance, and lifecycle management. The market is typically used by data science, ML engineering, platform engineering, and AI operations teams that need to industrialize AI workloads across production environments.
ai infrastructure and operations softwar.expandedDefinition:
Software used to build, deploy, operate, monitor, govern, and scale AI systems and machine learning workloads. This category includes the infrastructure and operational tooling that sits beneath AI-enabled applications, supporting model development, inference serving, orchestration, observability, governance, and lifecycle management.

The market is typically used by data science, ML engineering, platform engineering, and AI operations teams that need to industrialize AI workloads across production environments. Buyers evaluate these products on scalability, performance, reliability, governance, and the ability to move models from experimentation into repeatable production use across cloud, on-premise, and hybrid deployments.

productivity applications.definition: Applications used to support employee communication, collaboration, knowledge work, and day-to-day productivity.
productivity applications.expandedDefinition:
Applications used to support day-to-day knowledge work, communication, coordination, information creation, and organizational productivity across individuals and teams. Products in this market are designed to improve how employees communicate, collaborate, create content, manage information, and execute routine work within enterprise environments.

This segment generally includes two major subsegments:

Communication Applications — software focused on interpersonal and team communication, coordination, and collaboration, including messaging, email, meetings, video conferencing, calendars, team workspaces, and collaborative task management.
Content Management Applications — software focused on the creation, organization, storage, retrieval, governance, and publishing of digital content and enterprise information, including documents, files, records, knowledge bases, media assets, and web content.

The defining characteristic of productivity applications is that they primarily support horizontal employee productivity and information work rather than owning transactional business processes or operational systems of record. These products are typically used broadly across organizations regardless of industry or business function and are often deeply embedded into daily employee workflows.

Common capabilities across the segment include:

Communication and collaboration workflows
Document and file management
Knowledge sharing and search
Content authoring and publishing
Task and workflow coordination
Permissions and access controls
Enterprise search and information retrieval
Workflow approvals and lightweight process automation

This category is distinct from:

Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) applications, which manage structured operational and transactional processes
Customer Operations applications, which manage external customer-facing workflows
Analytics and BI software, which focuses on decision support and data analysis
Application development software, which is used primarily by engineering teams to build software

Products in this market are typically purchased by CIO organizations, workplace productivity teams, IT departments, operations leaders, and business function owners. Competitive differentiation is often driven by usability, collaboration depth, ecosystem integration, workflow flexibility, content governance capabilities, cross-platform accessibility, and increasingly AI-assisted productivity features.

customer relationship management (crm) a.definition: Customer Relationship Management (CRM) Applications includes customer-facing business applications used to manage commercial workflows across marketing, sales, commerce, support, and customer engagement. CRM applications centralize customer data and coordinate the processes that acquire, convert, support, and retain customers, making them the operational system of engagement for commercial teams. Products are typically differentiated by ownership of customer lifecycle workflows and integration with customer data systems.
customer relationship management (crm) a.expandedDefinition:
A horizontal application layer used to manage customer relationships, engagement, and lifecycle workflows across marketing, sales, and service. CRM applications centralize customer data and coordinate the processes that acquire, convert, support, and retain customers, making them the operational system of engagement for commercial teams.

This layer is defined by ownership of the customer record and the workflows that surround it: lead capture, account management, opportunity tracking, customer communication, support cases, and retention activity. The market is broad, but the core boundary is that CRM owns customer-facing relationship workflows rather than the transaction engine, back-office finance, or supply chain execution.

commerce applications.definition: A horizontal application layer used to run digital commerce transactions and commercial experiences across online and omnichannel channels. This category includes the applications that manage storefronts, catalogs, discovery, checkout, order handling, and the transaction experience between buyers and sellers, including both direct merchant commerce and multi-party commerce models.
commerce applications.expandedDefinition:
A horizontal application layer used to run digital commerce transactions and commercial experiences across online and omnichannel channels. This category includes the applications that manage storefronts, catalogs, discovery, checkout, order handling, and the transaction experience between buyers and sellers, including both direct merchant commerce and multi-party commerce models.

Commerce applications sit at the commercial transaction layer of the software stack. They are purchased to create, manage, and optimize the buying experience, while integrating with payments, fulfillment, customer accounts, and post-purchase operations. The market boundary is that these products own the commerce workflow itself rather than the financial processing layer or the supply chain behind it.

engineering and production applications.definition: Applications used to design products, simulate systems, manage industrial operations, coordinate production, and support engineering workflows in manufacturing and industrial environments. These products sit close to the product design and production floor and are typically evaluated on their ability to support technical design, plant-level execution, quality, and operational coordination.
engineering and production applications.expandedDefinition:
Applications used to design products, simulate systems, manage industrial operations, coordinate production, and support engineering workflows in manufacturing and industrial environments. These products sit close to the product design and production floor and are typically evaluated on their ability to support technical design, plant-level execution, quality, and operational coordination.

The market includes software that bridges engineering intent and physical production execution, often with heavy integration into CAD, PLM, MES, plant systems, and industrial data environments. Buyers include engineering, operations, manufacturing, and plant management teams that need software aligned to technical, operational, and production realities.

enterprise resource planning application.definition: Integrated systems of record used to manage core enterprise administrative and operational processes across finance, procurement, workforce, assets, and internal operations. ERP applications standardize transactional workflows, enforce controls, and centralize operational data so that the enterprise can plan, execute, and report across core back-office functions.
enterprise resource planning application.expandedDefinition:
Integrated systems of record used to manage core enterprise administrative and operational processes across finance, procurement, workforce, assets, and internal operations. ERP applications standardize transactional workflows, enforce controls, and centralize operational data so that the enterprise can plan, execute, and report across core back-office functions.

The defining feature of ERP is that it serves as the system of record for enterprise processes rather than as a point solution for a single workflow. Markets within ERP tend to be defined by the back-office function they own, which is why finance, HR, procurement, asset management, and internal operations are separated as distinct subsegments in the taxonomy.

ai-native applications.definition: Applications in which AI capability is central to the user experience and the primary reason the product is purchased, rather than an embedded feature layered into a broader application. This category includes software that uses generative or agentic AI to perform tasks, create outputs, or change how users interact with the product at the core workflow level.
ai-native applications.expandedDefinition:
Applications in which AI capability is central to the user experience and the primary reason the product is purchased, rather than an embedded feature layered into a broader application. This category includes software that uses generative or agentic AI to perform tasks, create outputs, or change how users interact with the product at the core workflow level.

The boundary is that AI is not just an add-on or supporting feature; it is the product’s main value proposition and organizing principle. Buyers typically evaluate these applications based on output quality, workflow automation, task completion, and how much they change the user’s working model compared with conventional software.

supply chain management (scm) applicatio.definition: Applications used to plan, coordinate, execute, and optimize the movement of goods, inventory, materials, suppliers, and logistics operations across supply chain networks. These products support sourcing, demand planning, inventory optimization, transportation, warehousing, fulfillment, and supply chain visibility, and they are typically used by supply chain, logistics, and operations teams.
supply chain management (scm) applicatio.expandedDefinition:
Applications used to plan, coordinate, execute, and optimize the movement of goods, inventory, materials, suppliers, and logistics operations across supply chain networks. These products support sourcing, demand planning, inventory optimization, transportation, warehousing, fulfillment, and supply chain visibility, and they are typically used by supply chain, logistics, and operations teams.

The market is defined by its control over physical flow and planning across distributed partners, facilities, and transportation nodes. It is distinct from commerce applications because it focuses on the movement and availability of goods after demand has been created, rather than on the customer-facing commerce transaction itself.

computing and storage software.definition: Infrastructure software used to provision, virtualize, orchestrate, and manage compute resources, storage environments, cloud workloads, containers, and operating environments. This market underpins the runtime layer of digital systems and is typically purchased to improve resource efficiency, scalability, resilience, and workload portability.
computing and storage software.expandedDefinition:
Infrastructure software used to provision, virtualize, orchestrate, and manage compute resources, storage environments, cloud workloads, containers, and operating environments. This market underpins the runtime layer of digital systems and is typically purchased to improve resource efficiency, scalability, resilience, and workload portability.

These products are used to allocate and control the technical resources on which applications depend, including virtual machines, containers, file systems, object storage, and cloud infrastructure control planes. The defining boundary is that this segment owns the compute and storage substrate rather than the application, network, or security policy layer.

security software.definition: Software designed to prevent, detect, investigate, govern, and remediate cyber threats while protecting enterprise users, devices, applications, identities, networks, and data. This umbrella category includes the products that implement security controls, manage security operations, and reduce cyber risk across the enterprise stack.
security software.expandedDefinition:
Software designed to prevent, detect, investigate, govern, and remediate cyber threats while protecting enterprise users, devices, applications, identities, networks, and data. This umbrella category includes the products that implement security controls, manage security operations, and reduce cyber risk across the enterprise stack.

The market spans prevention, detection, response, governance, and data protection, which is why its subsegments are separated by the security surface or workflow they own. Buyers typically include CISO organizations, SOC teams, identity teams, compliance teams, and platform security groups that need layered controls across a modern enterprise environment.

it operations management software.definition: Software used by IT operations teams to monitor infrastructure and applications, manage incidents and service requests, automate operational workflows, and maintain uptime, reliability, and performance across enterprise environments. The market includes observability, IT service management, AIOps, configuration management, and operations automation tooling.
it operations management software.expandedDefinition:
Software used by IT operations teams to monitor infrastructure and applications, manage incidents and service requests, automate operational workflows, and maintain uptime, reliability, and performance across enterprise environments. The market includes observability, IT service management, AIOps, configuration management, and operations automation tooling.

These products are typically bought to give IT teams visibility and control over the production environment and the service layer that supports internal and external users. The boundary is that ITOM manages the operational health of technology services rather than the development of software or the security governance around it.

networking software.definition: Software used to configure, manage, optimize, and secure network connectivity across enterprise, cloud, and edge environments. This includes tools for routing, switching, WAN/LAN management, software-defined networking, load balancing, DNS, network visibility, traffic engineering, and network policy enforcement. In the market, this category sits at the infrastructure layer and is typically bought by network engineering, infrastructure, and cloud operations teams.
networking software.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Infrastructure Software focused on managing, optimizing, securing, and orchestrating enterprise network connectivity across cloud, on-premise, hybrid, and edge environments. Products in this market are used by network engineering and infrastructure teams to administer routing, switching, software-defined networking, traffic management, DNS, WAN optimization, network observability, and connectivity policies.

This segment owns the communication fabric between systems, users, and devices. Buyers evaluate these products on uptime, throughput, policy control, automation, visibility, and the ability to support increasingly distributed and software-defined network architectures.

database infrastructure.definition: Foundational software that stores, organizes, processes, and serves structured data for applications and analytics. This includes relational and non-relational databases, distributed databases, database engines, database-as-a-service platforms, replication tools, query optimization layers, and related administration software. The defining characteristic is that the product is the underlying data store or database runtime, not the analytics or application layer built on top of it.
database infrastructure.expandedDefinition:
Software used to store, organize, process, index, and retrieve structured, semi-structured, or unstructured data for operational applications and analytical systems. Database Infrastructure serves as the foundational runtime data layer of the software stack, providing the persistent storage engines and query systems on which applications and data environments depend.

Products in this market include relational databases, NoSQL databases, distributed databases, graph databases, time-series databases, in-memory databases, transactional databases, and database management systems. Core capabilities typically include query execution, indexing, transaction management, concurrency control, replication, clustering, high availability, scaling, backup support, storage optimization, and runtime performance management.

The defining characteristic of Database Infrastructure is that the software functions as the primary operational system for storing and serving data in production environments. Applications interact directly with these systems to read, write, and update data in real time. The category is centered on persistence, query execution, reliability, and runtime performance of the data store itself.

Database Infrastructure is distinct from:

Data Management Software, which governs, transforms, integrates, orchestrates, and operationalizes data across systems rather than acting as the runtime database engine
Backup and Recovery Software, which focuses on resilience and restoration rather than operational query and transaction processing
Analytics and Business Intelligence Software, which sits at the reporting and analytical consumption layer above the database environment

Products in this segment are typically purchased by infrastructure teams, platform engineering organizations, database administrators, and application engineering teams. Competitive differentiation is often driven by scalability, latency, consistency models, availability, workload performance, cloud-native architecture, multi-region support, and operational efficiency under production workloads.

backup and recovery software.definition: Software used to protect enterprise data and systems by creating backup copies, enabling restoration, and supporting disaster recovery after outage, corruption, ransomware, or accidental deletion. Typical capabilities include snapshot management, backup orchestration, replication, retention policies, restore workflows, disaster recovery automation, and recovery testing. This category is defined by data preservation and restoration, rather than day-to-day storage administration.
backup and recovery software.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Infrastructure Software focused on protecting enterprise data and systems through backup, replication, disaster recovery, and restoration workflows. These products are designed to ensure data resilience and operational continuity following outages, corruption, ransomware attacks, accidental deletion, or infrastructure failures, and they typically provide backup orchestration, snapshots, retention policies, recovery automation, and failover management.

The market exists to make sure critical workloads can be restored quickly and with minimal data loss. Buyers usually care about recovery point objectives, recovery time objectives, immutability, ransomware resilience, testability, and whether the software can support hybrid and multi-cloud recovery architectures.

ai build software.definition: A subsegment of AI Infrastructure and Operations Software focused on model creation and training workflows. Products in this market are used by data scientists and ML engineers to prepare features, label data, run experiments, tune hyperparameters, train models, and manage development environments for AI and machine learning work.
ai build software.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of AI Infrastructure and Operations Software focused on the creation, training, experimentation, and development of machine learning and AI models. Products in this market are used by data scientists, ML engineers, and AI research teams to build models and prepare them for production deployment.

Core capabilities typically include model training environments, experiment tracking, feature engineering, dataset preparation, data labeling, hyperparameter tuning, notebook environments, distributed training orchestration, vectorization workflows, model evaluation, and collaborative ML development tooling. Many platforms also support foundation model fine-tuning, synthetic data generation, prompt engineering workflows, and integration with GPU and high-performance compute infrastructure.

The defining characteristic of AI Build Software is that it supports the pre-production creation and development stage of the AI lifecycle — where models are designed, trained, tested, refined, and validated before deployment into operational environments. These products are optimized around experimentation velocity, model quality, reproducibility, and efficient model development workflows.

This segment is distinct from:

AI Deployment Software, which operationalizes trained models in production environments and manages live inference serving
AI Management Software, which governs, monitors, evaluates, and controls AI systems after deployment
Application Development and Testing Software, which supports broader software engineering workflows rather than AI-specific model creation

Products in this segment are typically purchased by AI engineering teams, data science organizations, research groups, and platform engineering functions. Competitive differentiation is often driven by training scalability, experiment management, developer productivity, compute orchestration, model interoperability, and support for modern generative AI and machine learning workflows.

ai deployment software.definition: A subsegment of AI Infrastructure and Operations Software focused on operationalizing models in production environments. Products in this market provide model serving, inference orchestration, deployment pipelines, runtime scaling, GPU and compute management, and edge or cloud deployment features that make AI models usable in live workflows.
ai deployment software.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of AI Infrastructure and Operations Software focused on operationalizing, serving, scaling, and orchestrating AI and machine learning models in production environments. Products in this market are used to make trained models accessible to applications, workflows, users, and downstream systems through reliable, scalable inference infrastructure.

Core capabilities typically include model serving, inference orchestration, deployment pipelines, endpoint management, GPU orchestration, autoscaling, model routing, runtime optimization, edge deployment, API management, and production inference monitoring. Many platforms also support multi-model orchestration, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines, prompt routing, low-latency inference optimization, and hybrid cloud or edge deployment environments.

The defining characteristic of AI Deployment Software is that it owns the production delivery and runtime serving stage of the AI lifecycle — ensuring that trained models can be reliably deployed, accessed, scaled, and integrated into live business applications and operational workflows. These products are optimized around latency, reliability, throughput, runtime efficiency, and operational scalability.

This segment is distinct from:

AI Build Software, which focuses on model development, experimentation, and training prior to production deployment
AI Management Software, which governs and monitors deployed AI systems after they are operational
Computing and Storage Software, which manages generalized infrastructure resources rather than AI-specific serving and inference workflows

Products in this segment are typically purchased by ML engineering teams, AI platform teams, infrastructure engineering organizations, and enterprise IT functions deploying AI into production. Competitive differentiation is often driven by inference performance, deployment flexibility, orchestration sophistication, scalability, GPU efficiency, and support for complex multi-model AI architectures.

ai management software.definition: A subsegment of AI Infrastructure and Operations Software focused on the post-deployment governance and operational management of AI systems. Products in this market typically provide observability, evaluation, explainability, compliance, policy enforcement, lineage, performance monitoring, and AI risk management across deployed models and AI-enabled applications.
ai management software.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of AI Infrastructure and Operations Software focused on monitoring, governing, evaluating, securing, and controlling AI systems after deployment into production environments. Products in this market are used to ensure that operational AI systems remain reliable, compliant, observable, explainable, and aligned with organizational policies and performance expectations over time.

Core capabilities typically include model monitoring, observability, drift detection, performance evaluation, explainability, governance controls, policy management, audit logging, lineage tracking, AI security monitoring, bias detection, compliance management, and operational risk management. Many platforms also support human feedback workflows, red teaming, AI evaluation frameworks, usage analytics, and governance controls for generative AI and autonomous systems.

The defining characteristic of AI Management Software is that it owns the post-deployment governance and operational oversight stage of the AI lifecycle. These products are optimized around visibility, accountability, reliability, compliance, and risk management for AI systems already running in production environments.

This segment is distinct from:

AI Build Software, which focuses on model creation and training workflows prior to deployment
AI Deployment Software, which focuses on runtime serving, inference infrastructure, and operational deployment
Security Software, which manages broader enterprise cybersecurity rather than AI-specific operational governance and observability

Products in this segment are typically purchased by AI governance teams, platform engineering organizations, risk and compliance groups, security teams, and enterprise AI operations functions. Competitive differentiation is often driven by observability depth, governance sophistication, explainability capabilities, compliance tooling, policy enforcement, and support for enterprise-scale AI accountability and lifecycle management.

communication applications.definition: Applications used to facilitate real-time and asynchronous communication, coordination, and collaboration between people and teams.
communication applications.expandedDefinition:
Applications used to facilitate real-time and asynchronous communication, coordination, and collaboration between individuals, teams, and organizations. Products in this market are designed to support interpersonal interaction, information exchange, meeting coordination, and collaborative work execution across distributed workforces and enterprise environments.

Core capabilities typically include messaging, email, video conferencing, voice and telephony, team collaboration channels, meeting management, calendars, presence indicators, notifications, file sharing, collaborative workspaces, and lightweight task coordination. Many modern platforms also incorporate workflow integrations, enterprise search, AI-assisted meeting summarization, conversational copilots, and embedded collaboration features across business applications.

The defining characteristic of communication applications is that they serve as the primary interaction and coordination layer between users rather than functioning as systems of record for structured business transactions or enterprise data. These products are generally horizontal platforms used broadly across organizations regardless of industry or department.

This segment typically includes:

Team messaging and collaboration platforms
Enterprise email and calendaring systems
Video conferencing and virtual meeting platforms
Unified communications and VoIP systems
Internal communications and employee engagement platforms
Collaborative workspace and lightweight coordination tools

Communication applications are distinct from:

Content Management Applications, which focus on storing, organizing, governing, and publishing information assets and documents
Customer Experience applications, which manage external customer support and service interactions
ERP and operational systems, which manage structured business processes and transactions
Social media or consumer communication platforms, unless specifically deployed for enterprise workplace collaboration

Products in this market are typically purchased by IT departments, workplace productivity teams, CIO organizations, and operations leaders. Competitive differentiation is often driven by usability, real-time collaboration capabilities, reliability, ecosystem integration, interoperability, security and compliance features, cross-platform accessibility, and increasingly AI-enabled productivity and communication assistance.

content management applications.definition: A subsegment of Productivity Applications focused on creating, organizing, storing, publishing, governing, and retrieving digital content and enterprise information. Products commonly include document management systems, enterprise content management platforms, knowledge bases, digital asset management systems, and web content management platforms.
content management applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Productivity Applications focused on creating, organizing, storing, publishing, governing, and retrieving digital content and enterprise information. Products commonly include document management systems, enterprise content management platforms, knowledge bases, digital asset management systems, and web content management platforms.

The defining feature is that the software manages information assets and content lifecycles rather than transactional business workflows. These products help organizations control versioning, permissions, retention, search, publishing, and access to institutional knowledge and unstructured content across teams and departments.

marketing, advertising, and sales applic.definition: A subsegment of CRM Applications focused on pre-sale customer acquisition, demand generation, campaign execution, pipeline creation, sales enablement, and revenue growth. Products in this market typically support marketing automation, advertising operations, lead management, sales force automation, attribution, revenue operations, and forecasting.
marketing, advertising, and sales applic.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of CRM Applications focused on pre-sale customer acquisition, demand generation, pipeline development, and revenue creation workflows. Products in this market are used to attract prospective customers, convert interest into qualified opportunities, manage active sales processes, and optimize commercial performance across marketing and sales organizations.

Core capabilities typically include marketing automation, campaign management, lead generation, lead scoring, customer segmentation, digital advertising management, account-based marketing, sales force automation, pipeline management, opportunity tracking, quoting, forecasting, revenue operations, and sales analytics. Many platforms also support customer journey orchestration, attribution modeling, territory management, sales engagement, partner channel management, and AI-driven prospecting or conversion optimization.

The defining characteristic of Marketing, Advertising, and Sales Applications is that the software owns the pre-sale portion of the customer lifecycle — generating demand, managing prospect engagement, converting opportunities, and driving revenue growth before or during the purchasing decision. These systems are primarily optimized around pipeline velocity, conversion efficiency, customer acquisition, and revenue generation.

This segment is distinct from:

CX Applications, which focus on post-sale customer support, service delivery, retention, and customer success workflows
Commerce Applications, which own the transaction and purchasing workflow itself
Analytics and Business Intelligence Software, which provides reporting and analysis but does not operationally manage customer acquisition or sales execution workflows

Products in this segment are typically purchased by marketing organizations, sales teams, revenue operations groups, and commercial leadership teams. Competitive differentiation is often driven by automation sophistication, customer data integration, omnichannel engagement capabilities, attribution accuracy, workflow configurability, forecasting quality, and support for increasingly data-driven go-to-market operations.

cx applications.definition: A subsegment of CRM Applications focused on post-sale and in-life customer support, service delivery, customer success, and issue resolution. Products in this market typically support contact centers, ticketing, omnichannel service, self-service, field service, case management, and customer retention workflows.
cx applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of CRM Applications focused on post-sale customer engagement, service delivery, issue resolution, customer success, and retention workflows. Products in this market are used to manage ongoing relationships with customers after acquisition, ensuring service continuity, customer satisfaction, account health, and long-term retention.

Core capabilities typically include customer support and ticketing, contact center operations, omnichannel service management, case management, self-service portals, knowledge management, customer success management, onboarding workflows, service analytics, field service coordination, and customer feedback management. Many platforms also support AI-assisted support, workforce management, conversational service interfaces, account health scoring, and proactive customer engagement workflows.

The defining characteristic of CX Applications is that the software owns the post-sale customer relationship and service lifecycle rather than the demand generation or revenue acquisition process. These systems are optimized around customer satisfaction, issue resolution, retention, expansion, service quality, and long-term customer value after a customer relationship has already been established.

This segment is distinct from:

Marketing, Advertising, and Sales Applications, which focus on customer acquisition, pipeline generation, and pre-sale revenue creation
Commerce Applications, which manage the transaction and purchasing experience itself
Productivity Applications, which facilitate internal employee collaboration rather than customer-facing support workflows

Products in this segment are typically purchased by customer support organizations, customer success teams, contact center operations, service organizations, and account management teams. Competitive differentiation is often driven by omnichannel support capabilities, workflow automation, service analytics, AI-assisted support, customer engagement orchestration, and the ability to improve retention and long-term customer satisfaction at scale.

marketplace applications.definition: A subsegment of Commerce Applications that supports both single-merchant eCommerce and multi-party marketplace commerce experiences.
marketplace applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Commerce Applications focused on enabling and managing buyer-facing digital commerce experiences across online and omnichannel environments. This market includes software used to operate both traditional single-merchant eCommerce businesses and multi-vendor marketplace platforms, provided that the software owns the core commerce workflow through which buyers discover, evaluate, purchase, and manage products or services.

Core capabilities typically include storefront management, product catalogs, merchandising, search and discovery, pricing and promotions, shopping carts, checkout, customer accounts, order management, subscriptions, fulfillment coordination, and post-purchase commerce workflows. Marketplace-oriented platforms may additionally support seller onboarding, multi-vendor catalog management, commission and take-rate management, ratings and reviews, and multi-party order orchestration.

The defining characteristic of Marketplace Applications is that the software owns the customer-facing commerce transaction experience and operational buying workflow, regardless of whether the merchant model is:

Single-merchant retail or direct-to-consumer commerce
Multi-vendor marketplace commerce
B2B commerce and procurement
Subscription commerce
Digital goods or services marketplaces

This segment therefore encompasses:

Traditional eCommerce platforms
Marketplace commerce platforms
Omnichannel commerce management systems
B2B commerce platforms
Subscription commerce platforms
Digital marketplace infrastructure

Marketplace Applications are distinct from:

Payment Processors, which manage payment authorization, routing, settlement, and fraud infrastructure rather than the broader buyer commerce experience
Customer Relationship Management Applications, which manage customer lifecycle engagement outside the transaction workflow itself
Supply Chain Management Applications, which manage sourcing, inventory movement, logistics, and fulfillment network operations rather than customer-facing commerce interactions
Procurement Applications, which govern internal enterprise purchasing workflows rather than external buyer-seller commerce experiences

Products in this segment are typically purchased by retailers, brands, digital commerce operators, marketplaces, B2B distributors, subscription businesses, and platform companies. Competitive differentiation is often driven by storefront flexibility, checkout performance, omnichannel capabilities, merchandising sophistication, ecosystem extensibility, scalability, fulfillment integration, and support for increasingly complex commerce and marketplace business models.

payments processors.definition: A subsegment of Commerce Applications focused on enabling, authorizing, routing, settling, and managing digital payment transactions between buyers, merchants, payment networks, and financial institutions. Products commonly provide payment gateways, merchant acquiring, fraud detection, recurring billing, reconciliation, tokenization, settlement workflows, and multi-currency transaction support.
payments processors.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Commerce Applications focused on enabling, authorizing, routing, settling, and managing digital payment transactions between buyers, merchants, financial institutions, and payment networks. Payment processors provide the infrastructure and operational workflows that allow commerce platforms and merchants to securely accept, manage, and reconcile payments across online, in-person, and embedded commerce environments.

Core capabilities typically include payment authorization, gateway services, transaction routing, merchant acquiring, settlement and reconciliation, fraud detection, tokenization, recurring billing, chargeback management, compliance management, multi-currency support, and integrations with banks, card networks, wallets, and alternative payment methods. Many platforms also provide developer APIs, embedded payments infrastructure, risk scoring, and financial reporting tooling.

The defining characteristic of payment processors is that they manage the operational movement, authorization, and settlement of funds within commercial transactions rather than broader merchandising, storefront, or customer engagement workflows. These platforms sit within the transactional infrastructure layer of commerce ecosystems and are often deeply integrated into checkout, subscription, marketplace, and embedded finance workflows.

Payment processors are distinct from:

Marketplace Applications, which coordinate multi-party commerce ecosystems and participant interactions
eCommerce platforms, which manage storefronts, catalogs, merchandising, and direct merchant operations
Core banking or treasury software, which manages broader financial institution operations
Financial Applications, which manage accounting, planning, and enterprise financial administration rather than payment transaction processing itself

Products in this segment are typically used by merchants, marketplaces, SaaS platforms, financial technology providers, and enterprise commerce organizations. Competitive differentiation is often driven by transaction reliability, global payment coverage, developer tooling, fraud prevention capabilities, settlement speed, compliance support, embedded finance capabilities, and breadth of supported payment methods.

financial applications.definition: A subsegment of Enterprise Resource Planning Applications focused on accounting, treasury, financial planning, billing, tax, audit, and financial reporting workflows. These products are typically systems of record for finance teams and are designed to manage the transactional, control, and planning processes that underpin enterprise financial operations.
financial applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Enterprise Resource Planning Applications focused on accounting, treasury, financial planning, billing, tax, audit, and financial reporting workflows. These products are typically systems of record for finance teams and are designed to manage the transactional, control, and planning processes that underpin enterprise financial operations.

The market owns the financial backbone of the enterprise, including close, consolidation, controls, and reporting. Buyers are finance and controllership organizations that need accuracy, compliance, and visibility into enterprise financial performance.

human capital management applications.definition: A subsegment of Enterprise Resource Planning Applications focused on recruiting, onboarding, payroll, benefits, workforce administration, talent management, learning, and employee lifecycle workflows. Products in this market are used by HR and people operations teams to manage the workforce as an enterprise asset and to standardize employee-related processes.
human capital management applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Enterprise Resource Planning Applications focused on recruiting, onboarding, payroll, benefits, workforce administration, talent management, learning, and employee lifecycle workflows. Products in this market are used by HR and people operations teams to manage the workforce as an enterprise asset and to standardize employee-related processes.

This segment owns the employee lifecycle and the administrative systems that support it. The market is defined by workflows around hiring, paying, developing, and managing people rather than broader financial, procurement, or operational administration.

procurement applications.definition: A subsegment of ERP Applications used to manage sourcing, purchasing, supplier workflows, and enterprise spend control.
procurement applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of ERP Applications focused on managing the sourcing, purchasing, contracting, and supplier management workflows required to procure goods and services for an organization. Procurement applications are typically systems of record for enterprise purchasing operations and are designed to standardize spend management, enforce purchasing controls, optimize supplier relationships, and improve operational efficiency across procurement processes.

Core capabilities typically include requisition management, purchase order creation, sourcing and RFx workflows, supplier onboarding, vendor management, contract lifecycle management, approval routing, catalog management, invoice matching, spend analytics, procurement compliance, and procurement workflow automation. More advanced platforms may also support supplier risk management, procurement marketplaces, dynamic sourcing, procurement orchestration, and AI-assisted spend optimization.

The defining characteristic of procurement applications is that they manage enterprise purchasing and supplier-facing operational workflows rather than broader financial accounting, inventory operations, or customer-facing commerce transactions. These systems are primarily used to control organizational spend, govern supplier interactions, and operationalize procurement policy across the enterprise.

Procurement applications are distinct from:

Financial Applications, which manage accounting, treasury, budgeting, and financial reporting
Supply Chain Management Applications, which focus on inventory movement, logistics, warehousing, and fulfillment operations
Commerce Applications, which facilitate external buying and selling transactions with customers
Contract management software, when used purely for legal document workflows outside operational procurement processes

Products in this segment are typically purchased by procurement organizations, finance departments, operations teams, and enterprise shared services groups. Competitive differentiation is often driven by supplier network scale, workflow configurability, spend visibility, compliance controls, sourcing intelligence, ERP integration depth, and automation of complex procurement operations.

asset management applications.definition: Applications used to track, manage, maintain, and optimize the lifecycle of physical or digital assets. For enterprise software, this usually refers to IT asset management, enterprise asset management, or fixed asset tracking. Typical functionality includes asset inventory, ownership, depreciation, maintenance scheduling, utilization monitoring, lifecycle management, and compliance tracking. The key boundary is that the product manages assets as tracked objects rather than as procurement, finance, or operations transactions more broadly.
asset management applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Enterprise Resource Planning Applications focused on tracking, maintaining, optimizing, and governing the lifecycle of physical or digital enterprise assets. Products commonly support asset inventories, maintenance scheduling, utilization tracking, depreciation management, lifecycle planning, compliance monitoring, and service workflows.

This market owns the record and operating view of assets that the organization must maintain over time, such as equipment, facilities, IT assets, or fixed assets. Buyers care about lifecycle control, maintenance visibility, compliance, and the ability to link assets to work orders, financial records, or operational processes.

operations applications.definition: Applications that support the execution, control, and coordination of internal business operations outside of core financial, HR, customer, or supply chain functions. This is a broad operational category that often includes facilities, procurement support, internal service workflows, administrative operations, asset workflows, and process management. The common thread is that these systems manage recurring operational work for the enterprise rather than customer-facing or industry-specific workflows.
operations applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Enterprise Resource Planning Applications focused on managing internal operational and administrative workflows outside core finance, HR, or customer-facing processes. Products in this segment commonly support facilities operations, internal services, operational coordination, administrative process management, workflow approvals, and enterprise operational execution.

This is the catch-all back-office operations layer for recurring internal work that does not fit cleanly into a more specialized ERP subsegment. The key boundary is that the product owns an internal operating workflow rather than a customer-facing, finance, HR, or procurement-specific process.

genai applications.definition: A subsegment of AI-native Applications focused on generating, transforming, or summarizing content such as text, code, images, audio, video, or structured knowledge outputs. Products in this market are typically used for drafting, copiloting, summarization, creative generation, conversational interfaces, and search augmentation, and are purchased primarily for their generative capabilities.
genai applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of AI-native Applications focused on generating, transforming, summarizing, or synthesizing content through generative AI models. Products in this market are designed primarily to create outputs — such as text, code, images, audio, video, presentations, designs, or structured knowledge artifacts — in response to user prompts, contextual inputs, or embedded workflows.

Core capabilities typically include conversational interfaces, text generation, code generation, summarization, content drafting, image and media generation, semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), copilots, prompt engineering workflows, and contextual knowledge synthesis. Many platforms also support collaborative editing, multimodal generation, personalization, workflow augmentation, and integration into productivity or business applications.

The defining characteristic of GenAI Applications is that the primary value delivered by the product is generative output creation and knowledge synthesis. These applications are optimized around helping users produce, refine, or interact with content more efficiently through AI-generated responses and creative assistance.

This segment is distinct from:

AI Agent Applications, which are optimized for autonomous task execution and workflow orchestration rather than primarily generating content
Analytics and Business Intelligence Software, which focuses on structured analysis and reporting rather than generative interaction
Application Development and Testing Software, which supports broader engineering workflows rather than AI-native content generation experiences

Products in this segment are typically purchased by knowledge workers, developers, creative professionals, enterprises, and consumers seeking productivity enhancement, content creation, research assistance, or conversational AI experiences. Competitive differentiation is often driven by output quality, reasoning capability, contextual awareness, multimodal support, workflow integration, personalization, and model performance.

ai agent applications.definition: A subsegment of AI-native Applications focused on autonomous or semi-autonomous task execution. Products in this market use reasoning, planning, memory, and tool use to carry out multi-step workflows, interact with software systems, and perform actions on behalf of users with limited manual intervention.
ai agent applications.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of AI-native Applications focused on autonomous or semi-autonomous task execution, workflow coordination, and operational decision-making through AI-driven agents. Products in this market are designed primarily to perform actions, complete workflows, interact with software systems, and execute multi-step tasks on behalf of users with limited manual intervention.

Core capabilities typically include reasoning and planning engines, memory management, workflow orchestration, software tool use, API interaction, autonomous task execution, agent collaboration, event-driven automation, contextual decision-making, and persistent operational state management. Many platforms also support multi-agent coordination, browser or application control, human-in-the-loop workflows, task delegation, and dynamic workflow adaptation.

The defining characteristic of AI Agent Applications is that the primary value delivered by the product is autonomous execution and operational workflow completion rather than content generation itself. While these products may generate text or conversational responses as part of the interaction model, generation serves the broader goal of completing actions and coordinating workflows across systems and environments.

This segment is distinct from:

GenAI Applications, which are optimized primarily around content generation, summarization, and creative or knowledge outputs
Integration and Orchestration Middleware, which coordinates system workflows deterministically rather than through AI-driven reasoning and autonomous execution
Productivity Applications, which facilitate human collaboration and workflow management but do not autonomously execute tasks on behalf of users

Products in this segment are typically purchased by enterprises, operations teams, developers, and individual users seeking workflow automation, task delegation, operational augmentation, or autonomous digital labor capabilities. Competitive differentiation is often driven by reasoning sophistication, reliability of task execution, integration breadth, autonomy level, memory and context persistence, orchestration capability, and the ability to safely operate across complex real-world workflows and software environments.

grc software.definition: A subsegment of Security Software focused on governance, risk, and compliance management across enterprise policies, controls, audits, and regulatory obligations. Products in this market are used to document control environments, collect evidence, assess risk, manage policy workflows, and demonstrate compliance to internal and external stakeholders.
grc software.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Security Software focused on governance, risk, and compliance management across enterprise policies, controls, audits, and regulatory obligations. Products in this market are used to document control environments, collect evidence, assess risk, manage policy workflows, and demonstrate compliance to internal and external stakeholders.

The market exists to make governance and compliance operational rather than manual. Buyers typically include risk, compliance, internal audit, legal, and security governance teams that need a structured record of policies, controls, issues, and remediation activity.

iam software.definition: A subsegment of Security Software focused on authenticating users, governing permissions, and controlling access to enterprise systems and data. Core capabilities typically include single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, identity governance, privileged access management, directory services, user provisioning, and access lifecycle management.
iam software.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Security Software focused on authenticating users, governing permissions, and controlling access to enterprise systems and data. Core capabilities typically include single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, identity governance, privileged access management, directory services, user provisioning, and access lifecycle management.

This market owns the identity layer of security: who the user is, what they can access, and how that access is granted, revoked, and audited. It is distinct from endpoint and data security because the primary object of control is the identity rather than the device or the data itself.

security operations (siem/soar/xdr).definition: Software used by security operations teams to detect, investigate, correlate, and respond to cyber threats across an organization. SIEM products aggregate and analyze logs and events; SOAR products automate response playbooks and incident workflows; XDR products unify threat detection and response across endpoints, identities, networks, and cloud assets. This category is defined by operational threat detection and response, not by access control, governance, or point security controls alone.
security operations (siem/soar/xdr).expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Security Software focused on detecting, investigating, coordinating, and responding to cybersecurity threats across enterprise environments. The primary object managed and protected by this segment is the organization’s overall security posture and operational threat environment rather than any single asset type such as endpoints, identities, or data.

Products in this market aggregate and analyze telemetry from across the enterprise — including endpoints, networks, cloud systems, applications, identities, and security tools — in order to identify suspicious activity, correlate events, prioritize incidents, and orchestrate response actions. Core capabilities typically include security event collection, log aggregation, threat detection, behavioral analytics, incident investigation, alert correlation, response automation, case management, threat intelligence integration, and security workflow orchestration.

The defining characteristic of Security Operations software is that it functions as the centralized operational command layer for cybersecurity teams. These platforms are optimized around visibility, detection, investigation, and coordinated response across the broader enterprise attack surface rather than directly enforcing protection on a specific resource type.

This segment is distinct from:

Data Security, where the primary object of protection is sensitive enterprise data
Endpoint Security, where the primary object of protection is endpoint devices such as laptops, servers, and mobile devices
IAM Software, where the primary object of control is user identity and access permissions

Products in this segment are typically purchased by security operations centers (SOCs), incident response teams, security engineering organizations, and enterprise cybersecurity functions. Competitive differentiation is often driven by detection quality, telemetry breadth, automation sophistication, threat correlation, investigation workflows, and response orchestration capabilities.

data security.definition: Software used to protect enterprise data itself, regardless of where it resides or how it is accessed. This includes data loss prevention, data discovery and classification, encryption, tokenization, rights management, access monitoring, and sensitive data governance. The defining distinction is that the software’s primary object of protection is the data asset, rather than the endpoint, identity, or network perimeter.
data security.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Security Software focused on protecting sensitive enterprise data regardless of where the data resides, how it moves, or which users, applications, or systems access it. The primary object of protection in this segment is the data asset itself.

Products in this market are designed to secure structured and unstructured data across databases, cloud platforms, SaaS applications, endpoints, file systems, collaboration tools, and data pipelines. Core capabilities typically include data classification, encryption, tokenization, key management, data loss prevention (DLP), rights management, access monitoring, data discovery, retention policy enforcement, and sensitive data governance. Many platforms also support insider risk monitoring, data activity auditing, compliance reporting, and AI-driven data risk detection.

The defining characteristic of Data Security software is that protection policies persist with the data itself rather than being tied primarily to a device, network boundary, or identity layer. These products are optimized around controlling data access, preventing leakage or misuse, and maintaining visibility into how sensitive information is stored, shared, and consumed across distributed environments.

This segment is distinct from:

Endpoint Security, which protects the endpoint device as the primary security surface
Security Operations Software, which manages centralized threat detection and incident response workflows
IAM Software, which governs user authentication and access privileges rather than the data asset itself

Products in this segment are typically purchased by security, compliance, governance, and data protection teams. Competitive differentiation is often driven by classification accuracy, policy granularity, cross-environment visibility, encryption architecture, compliance support, and the ability to secure sensitive data across increasingly distributed cloud and SaaS ecosystems.

endpoint security.definition: Software used to protect laptops, desktops, servers, mobile devices, and other endpoints from malware, ransomware, unauthorized access, and compromise. Typical capabilities include antivirus, endpoint detection and response, endpoint management, device control, threat isolation, behavioral analysis, and device posture enforcement. This category is distinct because the endpoint device is the primary security surface, even though the product may integrate with broader security operations platforms.
endpoint security.expandedDefinition:
A subsegment of Security Software focused on protecting endpoint devices — including laptops, desktops, servers, virtual machines, mobile devices, and workstations — from compromise, malware, unauthorized access, and malicious activity. The primary object of protection in this segment is the endpoint device itself and the runtime environment operating on that device.

Products in this market are designed to secure devices where users interact directly with enterprise systems and where attacks commonly originate or execute. Core capabilities typically include antivirus and anti-malware protection, endpoint detection and response (EDR), behavioral monitoring, threat isolation, device control, vulnerability management, patch management, endpoint compliance enforcement, ransomware protection, and remediation workflows. Many platforms also support zero-trust endpoint controls, mobile device security, and cloud-delivered endpoint telemetry analysis.

The defining characteristic of Endpoint Security software is that it enforces security controls directly on the endpoint runtime environment in order to prevent, detect, contain, and remediate device-level compromise. These products are optimized around protecting the integrity, behavior, and operational state of endpoint systems used by employees, workloads, or enterprise infrastructure.

This segment is distinct from:

Data Security, which protects the information asset itself regardless of where it resides
Security Operations Software, which provides centralized monitoring, investigation, and response coordination across the enterprise
Networking Software or Network Security products, which protect traffic flows and connectivity layers rather than endpoint devices directly

Products in this segment are typically purchased by endpoint management teams, security operations organizations, IT infrastructure teams, and enterprise cybersecurity groups.
